---
title: Advanced file access control
format:
  html: 
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
---

Up to this point, you have worked with standard Linux permissions using the familiar `rwx` model. That is more then enough for most everyday tasks, but sometimes you need a bit more control.

In this chapter, you will learn SUID, SGID, the sticky bit, ACLs, and file attributes, and when to use each one.

# Beyond Standard Permissions?

Standard `rwx` permissions work well for most file and directory access needs. But sometimes, you need more than just read, write or execute, you need to control how a file behaves when it runs, or manage how users interact in share directories.

Linux gives you 3 special permission types that extend the basic model:

- Set User ID (SUID)
- Set Group ID (SGID)
- Sticky bit

These are known as special permissions, and they are used in very specific situations where standard permissions fall short.

Here is what they do:

- **SUID**: lets a program run with the permissions of a the file's owner
- **SGID**: makes sure files created in a shared directory inherit the directory group
- **Sticky bit**: prevents users from deleting each other's files in public directories like `/tmp`

These permissions are most commonly used in shared environments or for system tools that need elevated priviliges

## SUID (Set User ID)

The Set User ID (SUID) permission is used on executable files. When a file has the SUID bit set, it runds with the permissions of the file's owner, nt the user who launched it.

This is useful when a program needs elevated privileges to do its job, even if it is run by a regular user.

For example, take the `passwd` command, for example. It lets users change their own passwords, but the actual password data is stored in `/etc/shadow`, a file that only `root` can modifiy

It works because `/usr/bin/passwd` has the **SUID bit** set and is owned by `root`. That means when you run the command, it temporarily runs with `root` privileges, just long enough to make the requiered changes.

```bash
ls -l /usr/bin/passwd
```

Notice the `s` in the owner's permission field: `rws` instead of `rwx`. The lowercase `s` means the SUID bit is set and the file is executable.

```bash
-rwsr-xr-x. 1 root root 91424 Nov  3  2024 /usr/bin/passw
```

If the file was not executable you would see a capital `S` instead.

### Setting the SUID Bit

To set the SUID bit on a file you own (or have permission to modify), use:

```bash
chmod u+s filename
```
To remove it:

```bash
chmod u-s filename
```

You can also use numeric mode. The SUID bit has a value of 4 in the special permission slot, so:

```bash
chmod 4755 <filename>
```

This sets SUID along with `rwxr-xr-x` permissions

::: {.callout-caution}
SUID should be used carefully. A vulnerable SUID binary can be abused to gain elevated access.

Avoid setting SUID on scripts or on files in shared directories. Stick to well-audited system tools, and always double-check ownership and permissions before applying SUID manually.
:::

## SGID (Set Group ID)

The Set Group ID (SGID) permission behaves differently depending on whether it is applied to a file or a directory.

### SGID on executable files

When SGID is set on an executable file, the program runs with the **group** of the file, rather that the group of the user running it.

This is similar to SUID, but it affects group permissions instead of user permissions.

### SGID on Directories

This is where SGID is most useful. When applied to a directory, it ensures that **all files and subdirectories created inside the group ownership of the directory**, not the user's primary group.

That means if you have set up a shared directory for a team, everyone's files will stay under the same group so you don't need to manually adjust group wonership every time.

Let's say you team uses a shared directory at `srv/projects`, and it is owned by a group called `devteam`. You want anything created inside that directory (files, folders, scripts) to automatically blong to the `devteam` group, no matter who creates it.

To make that happen, you can set the SGID bit on the directory:

```bash
chmod g+s /srv/projects
```
After running that command, you will notice an `s` in place of the group's execute bit:

```bash
ls -ld /srv/projects
```

Like this:

```bash
drwxr-sr-x 2 user devteam 4096 May 21 14:12 projects
```

**Note**: If it is not installed in Oracle Linux 10 installed with `sudo dnf install acl` so you can check the hidden groups. In this way you can use **ACLs (Access Control Lists)**
